topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started early, a jarring intrusion into the quiet morning. I was watering my petunias, the gentle spray of the hose suddenly feeling insufficient against the rising tension in my chest. From across the shared property line, I could see Mr. Henderson wrestling with some lumber, his movements quick and somewhat aggressive. Every swing of the hammer seemed to echo too loudly in the stillness. I stopped mid-spritz, my hand frozen on the nozzle. My knuckles were white where I gripped the hose. I watched him, his brow furrowed in concentration, and a knot tightened painfully beneath my ribs. I needed to move, to do something, but my feet felt cemented to the damp lawn.

[story 2]

"Are you going to finish that soon?" I called out, my voice coming out higher than intended. I stood by the window, peering through the blinds at the construction site next door. The sight of the freshly dug earth and the stacks of wood made my stomach clench. I kept glancing over, my gaze darting from the fence posts to Mr. Henderson’s determined silhouette. A cold prickle ran down my spine, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I tried to take a deep, steadying breath, but it caught in my throat. I started pacing the length of my yard, my steps quick and uneven, unable to settle my gaze on anything but the progress being made across the boundary.

[story 3]

I heard the scraping of metal against wood, a sound that felt profoundly wrong in this neighborhood. I retreated indoors, pulling the curtains shut as if the light itself were too bright. My heart was thumping a frantic rhythm against my ribs, a rapid drumbeat I couldn't silence. I crept to the back door, peering through the gap in the curtains. Mr. Henderson was working late, the porch light casting long, stark shadows. He seemed preoccupied, his usual cheerful demeanor replaced by a grim set to his jaw as he secured a section of wire. I backed away slowly, my breath shallow, every muscle coiled tight, waiting for a movement that I desperately hoped wouldn't come.
